data.title: Surmodics Reports Third Quarter Fiscal 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Surmodics, Inc., a leading provider of medical device and in vitro diagnostic technologies, has released its financial results for the third quarter of fiscal year 2025, ending June 30, 2025. The company also updated its financial guidance for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2025.

Financial Highlights

Surmodics reported total revenue of $29.6 million for the third quarter, a 3% decrease year-over-year. Excluding the SurVeil™ drug-coated balloon (DCB) license fee revenue, total revenue increased by 1% year-over-year. The company reported a GAAP net loss of $(5.3) million, an improvement from the $(7.6) million loss in the prior-year period. Adjusted EBITDA was $3.4 million, up from $1.6 million in the previous year.

Business and Operational Highlights

During the third quarter, Surmodics launched the Pounce XL Thrombectomy System, which facilitates rapid non-surgical clot removal in iliac and femoral arteries. Additionally, the TRANSCEND clinical trial results were published, demonstrating that the SurVeil™ DCB is non-inferior to the IN.PACT™ Admiral™ DCB for safety and efficacy in patients with femoropopliteal arterial disease.

Medical Device segment revenue decreased by 5% to $22.2 million, primarily due to a decline in SurVeil DCB product sales. However, the Pounce Thrombectomy Platform saw a 35% growth in sales. The In Vitro Diagnostics (IVD) segment reported a 6% increase in revenue to $7.4 million, driven by growth across all product lines.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Surmodics is focused on long-term growth, expense control, and completing its pending acquisition by GTCR. The company has expanded its pipeline of device applications for its Preside™ hydrophilic coatings and saw its first customer begin early commercialization of a device coated with this technology. Despite higher merger-related expenses, the company achieved notable reductions in operating costs and expenses.

Management's Perspective

Gary Maharaj, President and CEO of Surmodics, highlighted the company's broad-based growth across its business segments despite lower SurVeil DCB revenue. He emphasized the strong market response to the Pounce XL Thrombectomy and the expansion of the Preside™ hydrophilic coatings pipeline. Maharaj also noted the company's efforts to respond to the FTC's administrative complaint regarding the pending acquisition by GTCR.

Future Outlook

Surmodics has raised its fiscal 2025 total revenue guidance to a range of $116.5 million to $118.5 million. The company expects fiscal 2025 GAAP net loss to range from $(1.70) to $(1.55) per diluted share, and non-GAAP net loss to range from $(0.35) to $(0.20) per diluted share. The guidance reflects the anticipated impact of lower SurVeil DCB license fee and product revenue.

SEC Filing: SURMODICS INC [ SRDX ] - 8-K - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Manufacturing Revival Sputters; Tariff Talks Turn To Exemptions
data.description:
Sponsored by

U.S. Manufacturing Revival Sputters; Tariff Talks Turn To Exemptions By Paul Berger

President Trump said his sweeping tariff regime would revive domestic manufacturing. So far, the sector is sputtering.

The WSJ's Chao Deng and John Keilman write that economic activity tied to manufacturing has shrunk for most of Trump's second term and that some corporate pledges to beef up U.S. factories may not come to fruition.

Even though effective average tariff rates are at their highest levels since the 1930s, some economists say the levies aren't high enough to bring companies back to the U.S. The unpredictability of Trump's trade wars is also making it hard for companies to decide on big capital commitments.

Other factors are hurting the sector, too. Whirlpool says its U.S. plants have made fewer products this year as Asian competitors accelerated shipments to the U.S. to get ahead of tariffs. Motorcycle maker Harley-Davidson and off-road vehicle manufacturer Polaris cited consumer uncertainty as a factor behind decisions to tamp down U.S. factory operations.

The White House touts nearly $2 trillion of manufacturing-related projects announced since the start of the year. Nearly 80 projects are included in that tally including factories and data centers - many of which could take years to materialize.

Apple says it will build cover glass for every iPhone and Apple Watch in Kentucky. (WDRB)

CONTENT FROM: PENSKE Gain Momentum. Gain Ground with Penske Logistics.

Markets shift. Disruptions happen. But progress doesn't have to stop. Penske helps build supply chains that stay strong under pressure and keep moving forward. With agile solutions, expert teams and advanced technology, we help you respond fast and keep advancing in any environment.

Learn More

Economy & Trade

U.S. trading partners are lobbying the White House for exemptions to sweeping new tariffs that went into force this week, as countries seek to muffle the impact on their economies of President Trump's push to reorder global trade.

The WSJ's Jason Douglas, Kim Mackrael and Gavin Bade report that the diplomatic efforts show months of trade talks are far from over despite the run of agreements trumpeted by the White House in the past month.

The European Union, Japan and South Korea are among those that have agreed to pacts with Trump, while behind the scenes their negotiators continue to argue for further relief for prized export sectors. Dozens of exemptions and carve-outs have already been allowed for products including Brazilian orange juice and Chilean copper.

With the president showing willingness to adjust tariffs spontaneously in pursuit of a variety of political goals, uncertainty over access to the U.S.'s vast domestic market is becoming a key feature of the emerging economic order.

Toyota Motor expects a $9.5 billion hit from tariffs. (WSJ) Sony Group raised its annual profit forecast to reflect a less severe impact from tariffs than previously feared. (WSJ) Crocs expects a $40 million hit from tariffs in the second half of 2025. (WSJ) Number of the Day Quotable In Other News

China's exports grew at a faster clip in July, showing tariffs so far haven't curtailed the country's export machine. (WSJ)

Companies that export chips to the U.S. from the European Union will face a 15% tariff ceiling . (WSJ)

Germany's goods exports to the U.S. slid further in June . (WSJ)

The Bank of England lowered its key interest rate for the fifth time in a year. (WSJ)

Chinese carrier Cosco has completed what it calls the world's first green ammonia bunkering operation . (Lloyd's List)

Cummins is delaying the launch of its next-generation engine citing uncertainty around federal tailpipe regulations . (Transport Topics)

The West Coast's Harbor Trucking Association tapped Robert Loya as its new chief executive . (Commercial Carrier Journal)

BNSF Railway and Union Pacific Railroad are adding new intermodal services in efforts to win freight from trucking . (Journal of Commerce)

About Us

data.title: Lamar Advertising Company Q2 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Lamar Advertising Company, a leading owner and operator of outdoor advertising and logo sign displays, has released its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30, 2025. The company reported a slight acceleration in revenue growth and highlighted a significant acquisition in the billboard industry.

Financial Highlights

For the second quarter of 2025, Lamar reported net revenues of $579.3 million, a 2.5% increase compared to $565.3 million in the same period of 2024. Net income for the quarter was $155.0 million, up 12.7% from $137.6 million in the previous year. Adjusted EBITDA also saw a 2.5% increase, reaching $278.4 million.

For the first half of 2025, net revenues totaled $1.08 billion, a 2.0% increase from $1.06 billion in the first half of 2024. Net income for the six months was $294.2 million, a significant 36.2% increase from $216.1 million in the same period last year, primarily due to a $67.8 million gain from the sale of Lamar’s equity interest in Vistar Media, Inc.

Business and Operational Highlights

CEO Sean Reilly noted that revenue growth was observed at both national and local levels. Additionally, Lamar completed a milestone acquisition in early July, marking the first-ever UPREIT transaction in the billboard industry. This acquisition included more than 1,500 billboard faces across ten states.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

On July 2, 2025, Lamar issued 1,187,500 Common Units of Lamar LP to the owners of Verde Outdoor as part of an acquisition deal. The assets acquired from Verde Outdoor include over 1,500 billboard faces across ten states. This strategic move is expected to enhance Lamar's market presence and operational capabilities.

Management's Perspective

CEO Sean Reilly commented on the financial results, stating, "Revenue growth accelerated slightly in the second quarter, with increases on both the national and local levels. Our pacings indicate further year-over-year improvement in revenues is likely in the second half of 2025, though perhaps not to the degree that we'd anticipated entering the year. As a result, we've slightly revised our guidance for full-year diluted AFFO per share from a range of $8.13 to $8.28 per share to a range of $8.10 to $8.20 per share.”

Future Outlook

Lamar has updated its guidance for the fiscal year 2025, now expecting net income per diluted share to be between $6.09 and $6.11, with diluted AFFO per share between $8.10 and $8.20. The company remains optimistic about its revenue growth and operational performance in the second half of the year.

data.title: It's Hurricane Season for Stocks — WSJ
data.description:
By Spencer Jakab

This is an online version of Spencer's Markets A.M. newsletter. Get investing insights in your inbox each weekday by signing up here-it's free.

At least smart stock-market strategists have weather forecasters to keep them company.

Take outlooks for the Atlantic Hurricane Season, which just began its most active phase. The current one is expected to be worse-than-usual with 12 named systems (the fourth, Dexter, formed this week), eight hurricanes and three major ones. Meteorologists consider things like water temperatures and wind conditions, including those in the distant Pacific (El Niño or La Niña), and determine probabilities.

Yet people will claim on the basis of a single season that they were "wrong." Years expected to be placid like 1992 have gone down in infamy with Hurricane Andrew, among the costliest and deadliest ever. The 2010 season was much more active, but no hurricanes made U.S. landfall.

Stock-market predictions are even less certain. For what it's worth, though, we're entering a perilous stretch. August and September combined is the only two-month period with negative returns for both the S&P 500 and global stocks over many decades. Post-election years have been especially lousy, according to Carson Investment Research.

Volatility traders also know to batten down the hatches this time of year. The CBOE Volatility Index, or VIX, typically bottoms in July and then rises in late summer and early autumn. That's true even if one excludes tumultuous September 2008, the month Lehman Brothers went bust.

Could this be a stormy season? There are no certainties, only probabilities based on past patterns and underlying conditions. But the water is very, very warm.

One measure is simply valuation. The U.S. market's cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio is higher than it has been 98% of the time and approaching its tech-bubble peak.

Another is contrarian benchmarks of risk appetite like the Levkovich Index. It's a blend of 10 sentiment indicators named for the late, great Citigroup strategist Tobias Levkovich. The gauge recently hit euphoria territory.

Another risk factor that's concerning: a rapid runup in margin debt, or borrowed money used to buy stocks. These debts aren't at worrying levels compared to the total value of equities, but the pace of increase is alarming.

The four past times margin debt grew so quickly in the preceding two months saw stock-market losses in the following one and two-year spans. The result: an average two-year loss of 30% for the S&P 500, according to First Trust Advisors.

Ah, but a rate cut is expected in less than six weeks-the equivalent of dropping a gigantic ice cube from space into the Atlantic. Right?

Maybe not. Northern Trust notes that stock volatility typically has risen a month before rate-cutting cycles begin and has remained elevated for about a year.

That's no reason to panic, but you might want to check your roof now for leaks.

ANI Pharmaceuticals Reports Record Second Quarter 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
ANI Pharmaceuticals, a diversified biopharmaceutical company, has released its financial results for the second quarter of 2025, showcasing significant growth and operational achievements. The company, which focuses on developing, manufacturing, and commercializing innovative therapeutics, reported record-breaking figures in several key financial metrics.

Financial Highlights

For the second quarter of 2025, ANI Pharmaceuticals reported:

  • Record quarterly net revenues of $211.4 million, marking a 53.1% year-over-year increase.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA of $54.1 million, up 62.8% from the previous year.
  • Diluted GAAP income per share of $0.36 and adjusted non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1.80.
  • Increased 2025 guidance with expected net revenues between $818.0 million and $843.0 million.

Business and Operational Highlights

The company's Rare Disease segment, particularly its lead asset Cortrophin Gel, demonstrated exceptional performance:

  • Cortrophin Gel net revenues reached $81.6 million, a 66.0% increase year-over-year.
  • ILUVIEN and YUTIQ net revenues totaled $22.3 million.
  • Generics segment net revenues grew by 22.1% to $90.3 million.

ANI Pharmaceuticals also launched a pre-filled syringe presentation of Cortrophin Gel in April, which has seen strong interest and demand.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Key strategic initiatives include:

  • Increased investment in Rare Disease sales and marketing activities.
  • Strengthening the sales team and refreshing marketing materials for ILUVIEN and YUTIQ.
  • Executing on the addition of the chronic NIU-PS indication to ILUVIEN’s label.

Management's Perspective

Nikhil Lalwani, President and CEO of ANI Pharmaceuticals, commented on the results: "We had another record-setting quarter for our Company, with all-time highs in net revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS, reflecting very strong momentum across our business units."

Future Outlook

The company has raised its 2025 guidance, now expecting:

  • Net revenues between $818.0 million and $843.0 million.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA between $213.0 million and $223.0 million.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP diluted earnings per share between $6.98 and $7.35.

SEC Filing: ANI PHARMACEUTICALS INC [ ANIP ] - 8-K - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: AMC Networks Inc. Reports Second Quarter 2025 Results
data.description:
AMC Networks Inc. has released its financial results for the second quarter of 2025, providing insights into the company's performance and strategic initiatives. The report highlights key financial metrics, operational achievements, and management's perspective on the company's direction.

Financial Highlights

For the second quarter ended June 30, 2025, AMC Networks reported:

  • Net revenues of $600 million, a 4% decrease from the prior year.
  • Operating income of $64 million, a significant increase from $11 million in the prior year.
  • Adjusted operating income of $109 million, down 28% from the previous year.
  • Net cash provided by operating activities of $103 million and free cash flow of $96 million.
  • Diluted EPS of $0.91 and adjusted EPS of $0.69.

Business and Operational Highlights

AMC Networks has made notable strides in its business operations:

  • Streaming revenue grew by 12% to $169 million, driven by price increases across services.
  • Expansion of FAST channels with the launch of 11 new channels on TCLtv+.
  • Renewed long-term affiliate agreement with the National Content & Technology Cooperative (NCTC).
  • Partnership with Runway to incorporate AI models and tools in marketing and programming development.
  • Record engagement and subscriber acquisition for Acorn TV during the "Murder Mystery May" event.
  • Theatrical debut of "Clown in a Cornfield" with the largest opening weekend in the company's history.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

AMC Networks has undertaken several strategic initiatives:

  • Issued $400 million of new 10.50% Senior Secured Notes due 2032.
  • Completed a cash tender offer to purchase $600 million of 4.25% Senior Notes due 2029 at a discount.
  • Repurchased $99 million of Senior Notes through open market transactions.
  • Voluntarily prepaid $90 million of borrowings under the Senior Secured Credit Facility.
  • Repurchased 1.6 million shares of Class A Common Stock at an average price of $6.48 per share.

Management's Perspective

CEO Kristin Dolan emphasized the company's commitment to its strategic plan focused on programming, partnerships, and profitability. Dolan highlighted the acceleration in streaming revenue growth, strength in content licensing, and healthy free cash flow generation. The company has increased its free cash flow outlook for 2025 to approximately $250 million.

Future Outlook

AMC Networks has provided an optimistic outlook for the remainder of 2025, expecting continued growth in streaming revenues and maintaining a strong focus on delivering high-quality content across all platforms.

SEC Filing: AMC Networks Inc. [ AMCX ] - 8-K - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Chinese villagers hit by worst floods in generations say they had no warning
data.description:
Key points:
  • China's Guangdong province hit by record rainfall
  • Residents in hard-hit village complain about aid response
  • China announces multimillion-dollar disaster relief package

The several hundred residents of Pingtou, a village in China's sub-tropical south, have seen plenty of typhoons and rainstorms over the years. But nothing prepared them for this week's flooding - the worst there in generations.

Knee-deep brown water still covered the main road into the village, in Guangdong province, on Friday as residents dragged damaged furniture and home appliances out of their homes, at least four of which collapsed in the downpour earlier this week.

"The older folks here say that in the 100 years we've been here, they've never experienced such flooding," said one villager aged in his 50s who asked to use only his surname Zhong.

Floodwaters have never before entered his two-storey house, but this time they surged in, wrecking many of his belongings. Water marks on the walls of nearby houses were more than a metre (3.3 feet) high.

It was not immediately clear if anyone had been killed in the village.

A record 622.6 mm (24.5 inches) of rain fell on Guangzhou, the provincial capital from August 2-6 - almost three times average monthly rainfall for the city in August. At least seven people were killed due to flooding there, state media said.

China has been battling with record rainfall in its north and south as well as prolonged heatwaves in its interior.

The government announced on Thursday 430 million yuan ($59.9 million) in fresh funding for disaster relief, taking the total allocated since April to at least 5.8 billion yuan.

But in Pingtou, villagers said they were not getting enough support from local authorities to deal with the aftermath.

Zhong said he was told by officials that there was no relief aid available to deal with the floods.

"There was not even a bottle of mineral water provided to us," he said.

'NO ALERTS'

Across Guangdong, 75,000 people were evacuated as a precaution, but several residents of Pingtou told Reuters there had been no alerts about flooding in the area - leaving them ill-prepared.

On the night of the heaviest downpour on Tuesday in Pingtou, 73-year-old Zhang was woken up by her worried daughter-in-law in the middle of the night and rushed over to the relative safety of her son's two-storey home.

When the family woke the next day, the roof of Zhang's house had caved in.

"I'd been living in that house for more than 50 years," said Zhang, as she stared at her household items coated in the debris left by the receding waters.

Just outside Pingtou, fish and duck farmer Hu Songlin said the deluge had swept away the fish in his ponds, estimating the immediate losses at about 120,000 yuan.

"Now we won't be able to earn a single cent," his wife Hua said.

Experts have linked China's erratic weather - including floods and droughts - to climate change.

"We say that global warming can lead to heavier rainfall, but there's only so much water," said Johnny Chan, a professor at the City University of Hong Kong's School of Energy and Environment.

"So if one area has more rain, another area will have less rain. So what we're seeing is that there will be places which are becoming wetter and there will be places which will become much drier."

($1 = 7.1827 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: India approves $3.4 billion payout to state-run refiners for cooking gas losses
data.description:
India has approved 300 billion rupees ($3.4 billion) compensation to oil marketing companies for losses incurred in selling subsidised cooking gas, Information and Broadcasting Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw said on Friday.

The compensation, approved by Prime Minister Narendra Modi's cabinet, would be paid to state-run Indian Oil Corp , Bharat Petroleum Corp and Hindustan Petroleum Corp in 12 tranches, according to a government statement.

It will allow state-run oil companies to continue crude procurement, debt servicing, and sustaining capital expenditure, the statement said.

"As gas prices are impacted by geopolitics, the subsidy will insulate the country's middle-class families from any negative impact," Vaishnaw said at a briefing.

The government also allocated 120.6 billion rupees towards a scheme to provide subsidised cooking gas connections to women from nearly 100 million poor households.

($1 = 87.6880 Indian rupees)

data.title: DDD INVESTOR ALERT: Bronstein, Gewirtz and Grossman, LLC Reminds Stockholders of 3D Systems Corporation to Contact the Firm Today!
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / August 8, 2025 / Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC, a nationally recognized law firm, notifies investors that a class action lawsuit has been filed against 3D Systems Corporation ("3D Systems" or "the Company") and certain of its officers.

Class Definition

This lawsuit seeks to recover damages against Defendants for alleged violations of the federal securities laws on behalf of all persons and entities that purchased or otherwise acquired 3D Systems securities between August 13, 2024 and May 13, 2025, both dates inclusive (the "Class Period"). Such investors are encouraged to join this case by visiting the firm's site: bgandg.com/DDD.

Case Details

The complaint alleges throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements regarding the Company's business, operations, and compliance policies. Specifically, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) 3D Systems had understated the impact of weakened customer spending on the Company's business, whileoverstating its resilience in challenging industry conditions; (ii) in addition, the updated milestone criteria in the United Partnership would negatively impact the Company's Regenerative Medicine Program revenue; and (iii) as a result, the Company's public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

What's Next?

A class action lawsuit has already been filed. If you wish to review a copy of the Complaint, you can visit the firm's site: bgandg.com/DDD. or you may contact Peretz Bronstein, Esq. If you suffered a loss in 3D Systems you have until August 12, 2025, to request that the Court appoint you as lead plaintiff. Your ability to share in any recovery doesn't require that you serve as lead plaintiff.

There is No Cost to You

We represent investors in class actions on a contingency fee basis. That means we will ask the court to reimburse us for out-of-pocket expenses and attorneys' fees, usually a percentage of the total recovery, only if we are successful.

Why Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman

Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC is a nationally recognized firm that represents investors in securities fraud class actions and shareholder derivative suits. Our firm has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors nationwide.

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn, X, Facebook, or Instagram.

Attorney advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

Contact

Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC

Peretz Bronstein or Nathan Miller

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0654 ET - The economic impact of U.S. tariffs on Swiss goods is likely to be severe, the Association of Swiss Chocolate Manufacturers, or Chocosuisse, says in a statement. The country is subject to 39% duties in the U.S., a higher rate than initially set by President Trump. Swiss politicians must implement an effective package of measures to provide relief to affected companies and reinforce the country's long-term attractiveness as a business location, the trade organization says. For many member companies, the new tariff rate represents a serious challenge and, without mitigating measures, will jeopardize sales and jobs in the medium term, it says. A coordinated and united approach by the Federal Council and the business community is essential, the organization adds. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0553 ET - Swiss watch manufactures will likely implement another round of price increases in order to mitigate the hit from President Trump's tariffs, Bernstein analysts write in a research note. Switzerland is subject to a 39% tariff rate on imported goods to the U.S., which is higher than the tariffs initially set at 31%. Swiss watch brands depend on the U.S. for around one fifth of their sales, Bernstein says, citing data from the Digital Luxury Group in Geneva. The measure will prompt more price hikes, but demand for Swiss luxury timepieces remains resilient in the U.S., the analysts say. Bernstein believes Swatch will be the most exposed to the levies, due to its high exposure to the U.S. Richemont appears less exposed, the caveat being Cartier, they say. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0520 ET - Switzerland's central bank is unlikely to take interest rates below zero, despite the serious blow the Swiss economy faces from high U.S. trade tariffs, says Karsten Junius at J. Safra Sarasin Sustainable Asset Management. The Swiss National Bank in June cut its key rate to zero amid strong demand for the Swiss franc and countervailing headwinds to the country's key exports. Those headwinds have increased after President Trump said he would slap unexpectedly high duties of 39% on imports from Switzerland. But despite that tariff blow, the SNB is likely to prove loathe to take rates negative, Junius writes in a note to clients. Swiss inflation is exceeding expectations, and a policy rate of zero already stimulates activity, he notes. Further rate cuts would, meanwhile, add fuel to U.S. accusations of currency-meddling by the SNB, Junius adds. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0508 ET - The dollar is only modestly weaker after President Trump nominated Stephen Miran to fill a vacancy on the Fed's Board of Governors, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. While Miran recently criticised the Fed for lowering interest rates and has called for an overhaul of the central bank, he's temporarily filling the role until the term expires in January, he says. The news is also offset by reports that Fed Governor Christopher Waller is the favorite to replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell. He has a more moderate stance on cutting rates compared to Kevin Hassett, another potential candidate, Pesole says. The DXY dollar index drops 0.2% to 98.209. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0458 ET - The cost of insuring euro-denominated credit against default using credit default swaps stay around their lowest levels this week. President Trump is due to meet the Russian leader President Putin and prospects of an end to the Russia-Ukraine conflict are supporting market sentiment, IG's Christian Henke says in a note. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index which tracks euro junk bond credit default swaps edges up 1 basis point to 271 basis points, only just above this week's low of 270bps, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. The iTraxx Main index of European investment-grade credit default swaps stays steady at 54bps. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0450 ET - President Trump has made a good choice in nominating his economic adviser Stephen Miran to the board of the Federal Reserve, writes Paul Ashworth at Capital Economics. Miran, head of the White House Council of Economic Advisers, will for now fill a vacancy on the Fed board in the short term, but could, Ashworth says, be renominated, especially if he leans toward the rate cuts that Trump loudly favors. The move comes as "a welcome surprise," Ashworth says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0406 ET - Oil prices are on track for sharp weekly losses on news that President Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin could meet as soon as next week and as U.S. tariffs weigh on the global economic outlook. In early trade, Brent crude and WTI are up 0.1% to $66.50 and $63.90 a barrel, respectively, but are down 4.6% and 7.7% on the week. A meeting to discuss a Russia-Ukraine peace deal would significantly reduce the geopolitical risk premium and possibly affect Trump's secondary tariffs on India, easing market anxiety over supplies. Meanwhile, fresh U.S. tariffs on major trade partners and weak U.S. economic data are fueling concerns over demand just as OPEC+ moves to fully unwind its largest tranche of voluntary output cuts. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0407 ET - The timeline U.S. President Trump's administration imposed on EU negotiators to strike a trade deal was invariably unrealistic, says Jay Truesdale, a former U.S. diplomat and CEO of Washington D.C.-based intelligence firm TDI. This is given the scope of the topics and the complexity required to address important details. Both sides reached a political agreement in late July imposing a baseline 15% tariff on most EU goods. However, finer details such as what precise goods would be exempted from that rate and an anticipated joint statement that outlines the main points of the trade deal are still being finalized. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

0406 ET - Bitcoin eases on profit-taking, though it stays close to a one-week high reached overnight. The rise came after President Trump signed an executive order that directs the U.S. Secretary of Labor to review the guidance around alternative assets. The order lays the groundwork to add cryptocurrencies, private equity, real estate and other alternative assets into U.S. 401(k) retirement accounts. Bitcoin rallied on the news but has since pulled back slightly as ongoing uncertainty over U.S. trade policy keeps investors cautious on risky assets. Bitcoin falls 0.2% $116,937 after earlier reaching a high of $117,964, LSEG data show.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0232 ET - The dollar falls after President Trump said he would nominate Stephen Miran to temporarily join the Federal Reserve's board of governors. Miram is chair of the White House's Council of Economic Advisers. He will take over the seat from Fed governor Adriana Kugler, who unexpectedly announced her resignation last week. Markets expect his potential alignment with Trump's push for lower interest rates, Danske Bank analysts say in a note. "Miran has expressed skepticism about the Fed's independence and has argued for stronger presidential control over the Fed Board," they say. He is also the author of the Mar-A-Lago Accord, a plan to devalue the dollar. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.196 after reaching a 10-day low of 97.945 Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0146 ET - U.S. core CPI likely rose 0.32% on month in July, which would lift the on-year rate to 3.0% from 2.9%, says Tom Kenny, economist at ANZ. The reciprocal tariff rates set by the Trump administration in early April have now been implemented for most countries. The effective tariff rate on imported goods is around 20% currently, and Kenny expects some impact from tariffs on inflation in July relative to recent months. Still, it is appropriate for the U.S. central bank to cut the fed-funds rate at the September meeting, given a recent cooling in labor-market conditions and a return of core services inflation to longer-run trends, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0041 ET - A potential 150% to 250% U.S. tariff on pharmaceuticals would have a limited impact on China's drug industry, Morningstar analyst Yurou Zheng says in a note. The proposed tariff has dragged down shares of drugmakers and sector companies globally. But Zheng notes that most of the Chinese healthcare companies under Morningstar's coverage either focus on domestic sales or supply the U.S. through licensing deals with American pharmaceutical companies, or already manufacture onshore. Sinopharm Group is Morningstar's preferred pick, given it is China's largest drug distributor. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Economic Hit of U.S. Tariffs on Swiss Goods Could Be Severe, Chocolate Trade Group Says — Market Talk
data.description: The economic impact of U.S. tariffs on Swiss goods is likely to be severe, the Association of Swiss Chocolate Manufacturers, or Chocosuisse, says in a statement. The country is subject to 39% duties in the U.S., a higher rate than initially set by President Trump. Swiss politicians must implement an effective package of measures to provide relief to affected companies and reinforce the country's long-term attractiveness as a business location, the trade organization says. For many member companies, the new tariff rate represents a serious challenge and, without mitigating measures, will jeopardize sales and jobs in the medium term, it says. A coordinated and united approach by the Federal Council and the business community is essential, the organization adds. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)
